Title: A Comprehensive Survey on TinyML
Authors: Youssef Abadade, Anas Temouden, Hatim Bamoumen, Nabil Benamar, Yousra Chtouki, Abdelhakim Senhaji Hafid
Technology Node: ARM Cortex-M MCUs, Edge TPU, FPGA-based Accelerators
Limitations: Provides a comprehensive survey of TinyML including hardware platforms, software frameworks, model optimization techniques, applications, deployment workflow, and research challenges.
Useful For: Survey paper only; does not propose a new hardware accelerator, RTL design, or ASIC implementation.
column15: TinyML enables low-latency, privacy-preserving, and energy-efficient AI on resource-constrained edge devices through hardware/software co-design.

Title: TinyML: Tools, Applications, Challenges, and Future Research Directions
Authors: Rakhee Kallimani, Arunkumar Bandi, Kiran K. Pattanaik, and co-authors
Technology Node: ARM Cortex-M, STM32, ESP32, Raspberry Pi, FPGA, ASIC
Limitations: Comprehensive review of TinyML deployment tools, software frameworks, hardware platforms, optimization techniques, applications, and research challenges.
Useful For: Survey paper only; no novel hardware accelerator or RTL implementation.
column15: Demonstrates that successful TinyML deployment depends on hardware/software co-design, lightweight frameworks, and efficient edge hardware.

Title: A Review on TinyML: State-of-the-art and Prospects
Authors: Partha Pratim Ray
Conference: Journal of King Saud University – Computer and Information Sciences
Technology Node: ARM Cortex-M, STM32, Arduino Nano, Raspberry Pi, GAP8, GAP9, ESP32, FPGA
Limitations: Comprehensive survey covering TinyML hardware platforms, software frameworks, optimization techniques, deployment workflows, applications, and future research directions.
Useful For: Survey paper; does not propose a new accelerator architecture or RTL implementation.
column15: Highlights the importance of hardware/software co-design, lightweight inference, model optimization, and dedicated hardware accelerators for TinyML.

Title: A Survey on Hardware Accelerators: Taxonomy, Trends, Challenges, and Perspectives
Authors: Biagio Peccerillo, Mirco Mannino, Andrea Mondelli, Sandro Bartolini
Technology Node: GPU, TPU, FPGA, ASIC, CGRA, PIM, Manycore Processors
Limitations: Proposes a comprehensive taxonomy for hardware accelerators by classifying over 100 accelerator architectures based on architecture, host coupling, software, and general characteristics while discussing trends, challenges, and future research directions.
Useful For: Survey paper; does not propose a new accelerator architecture or implementation.
column15: Provides an excellent architectural taxonomy and design principles useful for developing configurable and energy-efficient TinyML hardware accelerators.

Title: Accelerating CNN Inference on ASICs: A Survey
Authors: Mohammad Motamedi, Phillip Gysel, Venkatesh Akella, and others
Limitations: Comprehensive survey of ASIC-based CNN accelerators including architectures, dataflows, memory hierarchy, processing elements, optimization techniques, and performance evaluation.
Useful For: Survey paper; does not propose a new accelerator architecture.
column15: Provides an extensive comparison of CNN accelerator architectures and highlights dataflow, memory hierarchy, and PE design considerations essential for energy-efficient ASIC accelerators.

Title: Benchmarking TinyML Systems: Challenges and Direction
Authors: Colby R. Banbury, Vijay Janapa Reddi, Max Lam, William Fu, Amin Fazel, Jeremy Holleman, Xinyuan Huang, Robert Hurtado, David Kanter, Anton Lokhmotov, David Patterson, Danilo Pau, Jae-sun Seo, Jeff Sieracki, Urmish Thakker, Marian Verhelst, Poonam Yadav
Limitations: Introduces the TinyMLPerf benchmarking initiative, identifies challenges in evaluating TinyML hardware, proposes benchmark workloads and establishes a fair benchmarking methodology for TinyML systems.
Useful For: Position paper; does not propose a new hardware accelerator.
column15: Benchmarking is essential for comparing TinyML hardware and motivates standardized evaluation of future TinyML ASIC accelerators.

Title: A Machine Learning-oriented Survey on Tiny Machine Learning
Authors: Luigi Capogrosso, Federico Cunico, Dong Seon Cheng, Franco Fummi, Marco Cristani
Limitations: Presents a comprehensive PRISMA-based survey focusing on TinyML learning algorithms, optimization techniques, hardware platforms, software frameworks, ML/HW co-design workflows, and future research directions.
Useful For: Survey paper only; no novel RTL or ASIC architecture.
column15: Emphasizes hardware-software co-design and provides an extensive taxonomy of TinyML learning algorithms and deployment workflows.

Title: Tiny Machine Learning (TinyML): Research Trends and Future Application Opportunities
Authors: Hui Han and Silvana Trimi and Sang M. Lee
Limitations: Presents the first bibliometric analysis of TinyML literature using 392 peer-reviewed publications, identifying research trends, collaboration networks, emerging themes, and future research directions.
Useful For: Bibliometric study; does not propose hardware architectures or implementations.
column15: Provides quantitative evidence of the rapid growth of TinyML research and identifies future opportunities including sustainable hardware, federated learning, and privacy-preserving edge AI.

Title: Tiny Machine Learning: Progress and Futures
Authors: Ji Lin, Ligeng Zhu, Wei-Ming Chen, Wei-Chen Wang, Song Han
Limitations: Comprehensive review of TinyML evolution, deployment challenges, MCUNet, TinyEngine, on-device training, algorithm-system co-design, and future research directions.
Useful For: Survey paper; does not introduce a completely new accelerator architecture.
column15: Highlights that successful TinyML requires algorithm-system co-design and introduces MCUNet as a representative TinyML framework for efficient inference and training.

Title: DianNao: A Small-Footprint High-Throughput Accelerator for Ubiquitous Machine Learning
Authors: Tianshi Chen, Zidong Du, Ninghui Sun, Jia Wang, Chengyong Wu, Yunji Chen, Olivier Temam
Limitations: Proposes DianNao, one of the first dedicated ASIC accelerators for CNNs and DNNs with optimized memory hierarchy, delivering high throughput and energy efficiency in a compact footprint.
Useful For: Supports inference only and targets dense neural networks without exploiting sparsity.
column15: Introduced dedicated ASIC acceleration for deep learning and demonstrated the importance of memory hierarchy optimization in neural network accelerators.

Title: ShiDianNao: Shifting Vision Processing Closer to the Sensor
Authors: Zidong Du, Robert Fasthuber, Tianshi Chen, Xiaobing Feng, Yunji Chen, Paolo Ienne, Ling Li, Olivier Temam, Tao Luo
Limitations: Introduces ShiDianNao, a CNN accelerator integrated near the image sensor to eliminate DRAM accesses, significantly improving energy efficiency through on-chip weight storage and optimized data movement.
Useful For: Focused primarily on CNN inference for vision applications and lacks flexibility for broader neural network workloads.
column15: Demonstrates that minimizing off-chip memory access is critical for ultra-energy-efficient CNN acceleration.

Title: DaDianNao: A Machine-Learning Supercomputer
Authors: Yunji Chen, Tao Luo, Shaoli Liu, Shijin Zhang, Liqiang He, Jia Wang, Ling Li, Tianshi Chen, Zhiwei Xu, Ninghui Sun, Olivier Temam
Limitations: Presents DaDianNao, a scalable multi-chip deep learning accelerator that stores neural network parameters entirely on-chip using distributed eDRAM, drastically reducing external memory traffic.
Useful For: Requires multiple custom chips and is primarily intended for datacenter-scale deployment.
column15: Introduces scalable multi-chip neural network acceleration with distributed on-chip memory and high-speed interconnects.

Title: Eyeriss: An Energy-Efficient Reconfigurable Accelerator for Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
Authors: Yu-Hsin Chen, Tushar Krishna, Joel S. Emer, Vivienne Sze
Limitations: Introduces the Row-Stationary (RS) dataflow, a reconfigurable PE array, hierarchical memory system, compression, and data gating to minimize data movement energy.
Useful For: Optimized for CNN inference and dense workloads; limited support for emerging compact and sparse networks.
column15: Establishes Row-Stationary dataflow as a highly energy-efficient strategy for CNN acceleration through maximal data reuse.

Title: Eyeriss v2: A Flexible Accelerator for Emerging Deep Neural Networks on Mobile Devices
Authors: Yu-Hsin Chen, Tien-Ju Yang, Joel S. Emer, Vivienne Sze
Conference: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems
Limitations: Introduces Eyeriss v2 with a hierarchical mesh NoC and compressed-domain processing to efficiently support compact and sparse DNNs on mobile devices.
Useful For: Primarily targets inference and requires workload-specific mapping for best performance.
column15: Extends the Eyeriss architecture with flexible communication and native sparse computation support for modern compact neural networks.

Title: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a Tensor Processing Unit
Authors: Norman P. Jouppi, Cliff Young, Nishant Patil, David Patterson, et al.
Limitations: Introduces Google's first-generation Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) for datacenter inference, featuring a massive systolic matrix multiplication unit, deterministic execution, and software-managed memory to achieve high throughput and energy efficiency.
Useful For: Optimized for inference only and integer operations; lacks support for training workloads.
column15: Demonstrates the effectiveness of systolic-array-based architectures and dedicated on-chip memory for large-scale neural network inference.

Title: Simba: Scaling Deep-Learning Inference with Multi-Chip-Module-Based Architecture
Authors: Yakun Sophia Shao, Jason Clemons, Rangharajan Venkatesan, Brian Zimmer, William J. Dally, Joel Emer, Stephen W. Keckler, et al.
Limitations: Introduces Simba, a scalable chiplet-based deep learning accelerator using multi-chip-module technology and communication-aware mapping for high-performance inference.
Useful For: Primarily targets datacenter inference and depends on advanced chiplet packaging.
column15: Demonstrates scalable chiplet-based accelerator architecture and efficient communication-aware workload mapping.

Title: Origami: A 803-GOp/s/W Convolution Network Accelerator
Authors: Lukas Cavigelli, Luca Benini
Conference: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
Limitations: Presents Origami, an ultra-energy-efficient convolution accelerator optimized for embedded vision with fixed-point computation and reduced external memory bandwidth.
Useful For: Designed specifically for convolutional layers and embedded vision applications.
column15: Highlights aggressive energy optimization and bandwidth reduction techniques for embedded CNN inference.

Title: Building OpenLANE: A 130nm OpenROAD-based Tapeout-Proven Flow
Authors: Mohamed Shalan, Tim Edwards
Limitations: Presents the OpenLANE digital ASIC flow built on OpenROAD and Sky130, demonstrating fully open-source RTL-to-GDSII implementation through successful silicon tapeouts.
Useful For: Targets the Sky130 technology node and digital ASIC designs.
column15: Provides the complete open-source implementation methodology that forms the ASIC backend flow used in this project.

Title: From RTL to Fabrication: Survey of Open-Source EDA Tools and PDKs
Authors: Emilio Isaac Baungarten-Leon
Limitations: Comprehensive survey covering the complete open-source RTL-to-GDSII ecosystem including Yosys, OpenROAD, OpenLANE, OpenSTA, Magic, Netgen, KLayout and open PDKs.
Useful For: Survey paper; does not introduce a new implementation methodology.
column15: Provides a comprehensive overview of modern open-source ASIC design tools that support reproducible silicon design.

Title: OpenLANE: The Open-Source Digital ASIC Implementation Flow
Authors: Ahmed Alaa Ghazy, Mohamed Shalan
Limitations: Describes the OpenLANE automated digital ASIC implementation flow, including synthesis, floorplanning, placement, routing, clock tree synthesis, DRC/LVS verification, and SoC integration.
Useful For: Focused primarily on Sky130 and digital ASIC implementation.
column15: Forms the practical RTL-to-GDSII methodology adopted by this TinyML systolic-array accelerator project.

Title: Energy-efficient Acceleration of Convolutional Neural Networks using Computation Reuse
Authors: Azam Ghanbari, Mehdi Modarressi
Limitations: Proposes CORN-C, a computation reuse-aware CNN accelerator that exploits repeated quantized weights to eliminate redundant multiplications, reducing both energy consumption and execution latency.
Useful For: Effectiveness depends on weight quantization and redundancy in CNN filter values.
column15: Introduces computation reuse as an architectural optimization complementary to systolic-array accelerators for energy-efficient TinyML inference.

Title: Low Power & Mobile Hardware Accelerators for Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
Authors: Anthony G. Scanlan
Limitations: Comprehensive review of low-power mobile CNN accelerators covering dataflow, reduced precision, sparsity, memory hierarchy, analog computing, and neuromorphic approaches for embedded AI.
Useful For: Survey paper; does not propose a new accelerator architecture.
column15: Provides an extensive overview of architectural techniques used to reduce memory access and computation energy in CNN accelerators.

Title: Recent Developments in Low-Power AI Accelerators: A Survey
Authors: Christoffer Åleskog, Håkan Grahn, Anton Borg
Limitations: Reviews 79 low-power AI accelerators published between 2019–2022, analyzing power efficiency, precision, acceleration targets, systolic arrays, dataflows, and edge AI hardware.
Useful For: Survey only; no novel hardware implementation.
column15: Provides a modern overview of edge AI accelerator trends and serves as a benchmark for positioning new TinyML accelerator designs.
